experiment: F13 — Drosophila Larva Connectome — FPL Cross-Species Replication (Winding et al. 2023)
description: Cross-species replication test of the Functional Proximity Law (FPL) in the complete Drosophila melanogaster larval brain connectome (Winding et al. 2023, Science 379, eadd9330). Extends F12 (C. elegans, n=302, r=0.807, p<0.001) to an insect brain 10x larger (n=2952 neurons). If FPL holds (r > 0 between axon-to-dendrite and axon-to-axon synapse layers, p < 0.05), it is the strongest cross-species evidence to date that the Functional Proximity Law is a universal property of nervous systems, conserved across approximately 600 million years of evolution separating the nematode and insect lineages. Data: Supplementary Data S1 from Winding et al. 2023, connectivity matrices for axon-to-dendrite (ad, n~63k edges) and axon-to-axon (aa, n~40k edges) compartment synapse types. Layer definitions are from the original paper authors, not from IRDME. Pre-registered 2026-05-26 before any IRDME analysis was run.

hypotheses.statement: r(axon_to_dendrite, axon_to_axon) > 0 with p < 0.05 in the Drosophila larval connectome (n=2952 neurons). The Functional Proximity Law holds cross-species: neurons that form many conventional axon-to-dendrite synapses also form many axon-to-axon connections, because both layer types encode direct physical neuron-to-neuron coupling (same relational regime). This replicates F12 (C. elegans, r=0.807) in an insect brain 10x larger. The larger sample provides strong statistical power. min_abs_r is set conservatively at 0.20 to account for increased biological heterogeneity (mixed cell types: sensory, motor, interneuron, mushroom body Kenyon cells) expected in a full insect brain vs the C. elegans hermaphrodite nervous system.

r(axon_to_dendrite, axon_to_axon) >= 0.40 in the Drosophila larval connectome. The Functional Proximity Law holds with meaningful effect size. Across all prior IRDME experiments in neuroscience (M_EXT2: r=0.7774, F12: r=0.807, M_MED1_h1: r=0.408), the cross-layer hub correlation in biological networks has consistently been moderate to strong. The threshold r=0.40 is a conservative lower bound representing a medium effect size (Cohen's convention), well below the C. elegans value of 0.807 but above trivial correlation. CONFIRMED would mean FPL is not merely directionally conserved across species but shows substantial structural conservation. DENIED would suggest that greater biological complexity in the insect brain reduces hub persistence below the medium-effect threshold, which is scientifically interesting as a boundary condition finding.

There exist at least 5 neurons that are structural divergents (high rank in axon_to_dendrite but low rank in axon_to_axon, or vice versa) — i.e., neurons with layer-specific roles that violate local hub persistence. This tests the complementary claim: even if FPL holds globally (h1/h2), the insect brain should contain specialized neurons that exploit the two synapse compartment types asymmetrically. In C. elegans, AVAR (rank 1 in chemical_synapse, rank 2 in gap_junction) shows near-perfect hub persistence. In a more complex brain with 2952 neurons and diverse cell types (sensory, motor, Kenyon cells, mushroom body output neurons), some neurons will show strong layer specialization. This is not in tension with FPL — it tests whether hub persistence is universal OR whether some neuron classes are exempt. If DENIED (fewer than 5 divergents), it implies extreme conservation across all cell types, which would be remarkable.
